bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Proprium Sanctorum: Zusatz-Homilien des Ms. Vernon fol. CCXV ff. zur nördlichen Sammlung der Dominicalia evangelia,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 81 (42. Jahrgang, 1888): 83-114, 299-321.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen
bibliographic.title: Proprium Sanctorum: Zusatz-Homilien des Ms. Vernon fol. CCXV ff. zur nördlichen Sammlung der Dominicalia evangelia

entities.label: Carl Horstmann's 1888 Proprium Sanctorum selection
notes: This is the immediate printed source represented by CME00064.

The first run ends on p. 114 with the continuation notice Schluss folgt; the article resumes on p. 299 and its target text ends on p. 321. The following cue Hou a man schal lyue parfytly introduces another manuscript work and is not part of this selection.

Horstmann prints twenty-eight full homilies, retains six incipit cross-references in their manuscript positions, and editorially omits the full repeated Erat homo homily for the Invention of the Cross.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1888 and its editor died in 1912; the historical edition is public domain by age. Modern scan rights and terms remain separate.
rights.component: 1888 transcription, selection, and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions using ordinary author-term/publication-term copyright rules

access.entityLabel: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

entities.description: A monumental late-fourteenth-century parchment codex. The complete supplementary sanctorale sequence is at current fols. 215v-227v; the twenty-eight homilies printed by Horstmann occur there around one full omitted homily and six internal cross-references.

holding.notes: The current record tentatively gives England, West Midlands; origin and patronage remain debated.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413, with 71 leaves thought lost
physicalDescription.format: Large codex, normally quires of eight; nineteenth-century crushed russet leather over bevelled oak boards
notes: The target sequence occupies a small portion of a codex containing more than 350 texts; it begins on a leaf also ending Piers Toller and ends on a leaf also beginning How a Man Shall Live Perfectly.

rights.basis: The manuscript text and anonymous late-medieval works predate copyright by centuries; this does not determine rights in modern photography, transcription, or editorial matter.
rights.component: Medieval text and underlying homily sequence

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service online order form; request a quotation for expert color photography of the entire surviving object
access.contact: Use the online order form; written-permission enquiries: permissions@bl.uk; the digitisation page also directs reproduction licensing questions to licensing@bl.uk
access.entityLabel: London, British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)
access.format: Ask for color TIFFs or highest-resolution archival image files; rare material may require expert photography rather than scanning
notes: Request the whole physical object: covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, all beginning and end leaves, every surviving manuscript leaf recto and verso, five inserted modern paper leaves, flyleaves and blanks, and edges when the service offers them. Identify the work separately at fols. 19v(a)-30v(a).

A rough standard-rate baseline for 412 leaf-side surfaces is about GBP 3,007.45 (first TIFF GBP 27.70 plus 411 subsequent images at GBP 7.25), before binding furniture, expert photography, handling, administration, or complex-request fees. Only a quote can establish the actual whole-object price.
British Library terms state that supplied images may not be copied or reproduced without written permission; request separate permission for any repository publication or commercial use.

rights.basis: British Library imaging terms state that requested images may not be copied or reproduced without written permission; additional permission and fees may apply.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and British Library supply/permission terms
notes: Ask permissions@bl.uk expressly about private research use, publication of images in the git repository, and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand use.

entities.description: A very large late-fourteenth-century English parchment miscellany preserving a parallel supplementary sanctorale sequence at fols. 19v(a)-30v(a), copied by Add Scribe 1. Current catalog structure moves from the Nativity of the Virgin homily to Matthew and appears not to contain the Vernon Exaltation of the Cross homily in this sequence.

holding.notes: Origin is given as England, perhaps north Worcestershire.

entities.label: London, British Library, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: 172 folios plus unfoliated modern paper leaves and five inserted modern paper leaves; many leaves lost or remounted
physicalDescription.format: Very large miscellany copied by three scribes
notes: Target execution is Add Scribe 1, fols. 1r-61v. Add Scribe 2, not the target scribe, is identified by the current catalog as Vernon Scribe 2.

Reassess Horstmann's mere-copy claim using current scribal assignments

next_steps: Inspect complete images around fols. 27v-28r

next_steps: Search Horstmann's editorial prefaces for a convention statement

scope.notes: Horstmann calls Add MS 22283 a mere copy of Vernon and says the Proprium is complete and verbatim. relations.objectLabel: Carl Horstmann's Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon (1877)
relations.subjectLabel: BSB complete IIIF sequence for Archiv volume 57 (1877)

reviewNotes: The medieval sequence, each manuscript witness, Horstmann's editions, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and modern facsimiles are modeled as separate layers.

Complete local facsimile bundles retain the provider's entire physical or bibliographic object. work_portion locators point to the corpus selection without redefining a full manuscript or journal as a cropped extract.
The article's traditional page citation is corrected to pp. 83-114 and 299-321. Printed pp. 322-326 are unrelated reviews.

Horstmann's 1881 claim that Add MS 22283 is a mere copy of Vernon is retained as a historical, tentative claim. Current catalog data assign the target sequence to Add Scribe 1; the scribe shared with Vernon is Add Scribe 2 elsewhere in the codex.

The XML's 2016 revision note reports removal of fragments from the middle and end, but does not identify them. No claim equates those unspecified fragments with the demonstrable Horstmann omission of Erat homo.

lineage.summary: The repository XML represents University of Michigan CME item CME00064, an encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1888 edition of a late-fourteenth-century supplementary sanctorale homily sequence in the Vernon Manuscript. Horstmann prints twenty-eight complete Middle English verse homilies in two journal runs, pp. 83-114 and 299-321. The manuscript sequence at Vernon fols. 215v-227v has thirty-five feast positions: twenty-nine full homilies and six title-only cross-references. Horstmann retains the six references but omits the full Invention of the Cross homily beginning Erat homo; the encoded selection therefore must not be mistaken for the entire manuscript sequence. The complete 710-canvas Vernon object, not a cropped work extract, is cached and the selected span is mapped within it. The complete 4,616,920,131-byte Oxford facsimile/transcription dataset is also cached and integrity-checked, with exact internal transcription and image paths recorded for fols. 215v-227v. The parallel sequence in British Library Add MS 22283 is collateral evidence, not a documented source for the 1888 transcription; current catalog structure places it at fols. 19v(a)-30v(a) and appears to omit the Exaltation of the Cross homily present in Vernon.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous prologue poet and anonymous homilist or homilists
catalogSummary.date: Supplementary homilies composed in the late fourteenth century; parallel Add MS 22283 witness dated 1360-1399; Vernon witness dated 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; selection published 1888
catalogSummary.region: First Vernon/Simeon expansion transmitted in a Midland dialect; Horstmann tentatively localized the prologue to northwestern England and the remaining additions to southern England
catalogSummary.title: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Proprium Sanctorum: Supplementary Homilies from the Vernon Manuscript (Horstmann selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Archiv 81 (1888), pp. 83-114 and 299-321, and represented as CME00064: twenty-eight complete anonymous Middle English verse homilies from the Vernon Manuscript supplementary sanctorale, six internal title-only cross-references retained from the manuscript sequence, Latin Gospel rubrics, and German editorial framing and notes. It is not one medieval poem, not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, not the entire Vernon supplementary sequence, and not the whole Vernon Manuscript.

contentParts.label: Horstmann's supplementary sanctorale selection

locators.value: sequence fols. 215v-227v, canvases 318-342; full Erat homo at fols. 221r-222r omitted
parallel sequence fols. 19v(a)-30v(a); current catalog appears to omit the Exaltation of the Cross homily

contentParts.summary: Twenty-eight complete verse homilies selected from a thirty-five-position Vernon feast sequence, including its distinct prologue but excluding the repeated full Erat homo homily.

contentParts.label: 1. Nisi granum: grain-of-wheat prologue
contentParts.summary: A 251-line prologue on Christ as the grain of wheat, the Passion, Eucharistic bread, judgment, and salvation; Horstmann treats it as distinct from the feast sequence proper.

contentParts.label: 2. Stabat Johannes: Vigil of Saint Andrew
contentParts.summary: A 190-line vigil homily beginning Stabat Johannes, in two verse groups.

contentParts.label: 3. Ambulans Jesus: Saint Andrew
contentParts.summary: A 250-line homily for Saint Andrew beginning Ambulans Jesus, in two verse groups.

contentParts.label: 4. Liber generationis: Conception of the Virgin
contentParts.summary: A 182-line homily for the Conception of the Virgin beginning Liber generationis.

contentParts.label: 5. Thomas unus: Saint Thomas the Apostle
contentParts.summary: A 208-line homily for Saint Thomas the Apostle beginning Thomas unus.

contentParts.label: 6. Dicebat Jesus: Saint Stephen
contentParts.summary: A 382-line homily for Saint Stephen beginning Dicebat Jesus.

contentParts.label: 7. Dixit Jesus Petro: Saint John the Apostle
contentParts.summary: A 184-line homily for Saint John the Apostle beginning Dixit Jesus Petro.

contentParts.label: 8. Angelus Domini apparuit: Holy Innocents
contentParts.summary: A 332-line homily for the Holy Innocents beginning Angelus Domini apparuit.

contentParts.label: 9. Nisi granum: Saint Thomas Becket
contentParts.summary: A ninety-six-line homily for Saint Thomas Becket beginning Nisi granum.

contentParts.label: 10. Ecce nos relinquimus: Conversion of Saint Paul
contentParts.summary: A 218-line homily for the Conversion of Saint Paul beginning Ecce nos relinquimus.

contentParts.label: 11. Venit Jesus: Chair of Saint Peter
notes: A title-only Purification pointer precedes this full homily in the manuscript sequence and is not counted as a separate content part.
contentParts.summary: A 225-line homily for the Chair of Saint Peter beginning Venit Jesus.

contentParts.label: 12. Confitebor tibi: Saint Matthias
contentParts.summary: A 114-line homily for Saint Matthias beginning Confitebor tibi.

contentParts.label: 13. Non turbetur: Saints Philip and James
notes: A title-only Annunciation pointer precedes this homily. The full Erat homo Invention of the Cross homily follows it in both manuscripts but is omitted from Horstmann's selection; a further title-only pointer for John before the Latin Gate then precedes part 14.
contentParts.summary: A 128-line homily for Saints Philip and James beginning Non turbetur.

contentParts.label: 14. Hoc est preceptum: Vigil of Saints Peter and Paul
contentParts.summary: A 224-line vigil homily for Saints Peter and Paul beginning Hoc est preceptum.

contentParts.label: 15. Jussit Herodes: Octave of Saints Peter and Paul
notes: A title-only Saints Peter and Paul pointer precedes this full octave homily.
contentParts.summary: A 164-line homily for the Octave of Saints Peter and Paul beginning Jussit Herodes.

contentParts.label: 16. Simile est regnum: Saint Margaret
contentParts.summary: A 138-line homily for Saint Margaret beginning Simile est regnum; p. 114 ends with the article continuation notice.

contentParts.label: 17. Rogabat Jesus: Saint Mary Magdalene
contentParts.summary: A 384-line Mary Magdalene homily with six separately headed exempla; lines 23-100 parallel Horstmann's first 1877 Gospel story.

contentParts.label: 18. Accessit mater: Saint James the Greater
contentParts.summary: A 144-line homily for Saint James the Greater beginning Accessit mater.

contentParts.label: 19. Intravit Jesus: Assumption of the Virgin
notes: A title-only Lawrence pointer precedes this full homily in the manuscript sequence.
contentParts.summary: A 116-line homily for the Assumption of the Virgin beginning Intravit Jesus.

contentParts.label: 20. Facta est contentio: Saint Bartholomew
contentParts.summary: A 214-line homily for Saint Bartholomew beginning Facta est contentio, in three verse groups.

contentParts.label: 21. Nunc iudicium: Exaltation of the Holy Cross
notes: A title-only Nativity of the Virgin pointer precedes this homily. Current Add MS 22283 catalog structure has no corresponding Nunc iudicium item and moves directly to Matthew at fol. 28r; verify against images before treating it as a definitive witness lacuna.
contentParts.summary: A 162-line homily for the Exaltation of the Holy Cross beginning Nunc iudicium.

contentParts.label: 22. Vidit Jesus hominem sedentem: Saint Matthew
contentParts.summary: A 172-line homily for Saint Matthew beginning Vidit Jesus hominem sedentem.

contentParts.label: 23. Quis putas maior: Saint Michael
contentParts.summary: A 212-line homily for Saint Michael beginning Quis putas maior.

contentParts.label: 24. Designavit Dominus: Saint Luke
contentParts.summary: A 160-line homily for Saint Luke beginning Designavit Dominus.

contentParts.label: 25. Hec mando vobis: Saints Simon and Jude
contentParts.summary: A 216-line homily for Saints Simon and Jude beginning Hec mando vobis.

contentParts.label: 26. Videns Jesus turbas: All Saints
contentParts.summary: A 180-line homily for All Saints beginning Videns Jesus turbas.

contentParts.label: 27. Vos estis sal terre: for a Doctor or Confessor
notes: The manuscript itself does not supply a feast label; avoid presenting Saint Martin as certain.
contentParts.summary: A 140-line common homily for a doctor or confessor, tentatively associated by Horstmann with Saint Martin, in three verse groups.

contentParts.label: 28. Egressus Jesus: Dedication of a Church
notes: The ending canvas and folio also begin the separate following work How a Man Shall Live Perfectly; the cue after Amen is not part of this homily.
contentParts.summary: A 196-line homily for the dedication of a church beginning Egressus Jesus, in three verse groups.

scope.description: Horstmann's edited selection represented by CME00064

extent.description: One cataloging root plus twenty-eight complete verse homilies. The XML contains 5,582 L elements in sixty-four LG elements. Six manuscript title-only references are embedded within the relevant homily sequence rather than counted as additional content parts. The full Erat homo manuscript homily is excluded from the edited selection.

formStatements.description: The medieval content consists of 5,582 rhyming Middle English lines in sixty-four verse groups. Latin rubrics and brief German editorial prose do not make the cataloged medieval selection substantively mixed-form.

genres.label: Edited selection
notes: The selection omits one full repeated manuscript homily while retaining six title-only cross-references.

scope.description: Six separately headed illustrative narratives within the Mary Magdalene homily
Horstmann's 1888 printed editorial layer

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The Vernon/Simeon expansion is transmitted in a Midland dialect; Horstmann's regional composition hypotheses are modeled separately.

scope.description: All twenty-eight complete medieval homilies
Gospel incipits, biblical references, liturgical cues, and formulae

languageStatements.label: German
notes: The XML legacy code ger maps to ISO 639-3 deu in this manifest.

scope.description: Article title, headnote, continuation labels, and editorial notes

notes: Feast labels are editorial identifications: Horstmann states that the manuscript itself does not name the feasts.
The six title-only references belong to manuscript navigation and are retained in the edition, but they are not counted as independent full homilies in this content structure.
Erat homo is a complete homily in both Vernon and Add MS 22283, not a title-only reference. Its full text is absent because Horstmann editorially omitted the repeated Temporale homily.
Part 21 has no Add MS 22283 locator because the current catalog appears to omit it; absence awaits image verification.
The article's correct target pagination is 83-114 and 299-321. Printed pp. 322-326 are unrelated reviews and are not part of this work.
The work is verse for cataloging purposes. Brief German editorial prose and Latin rubrics are language/editorial layers, not evidence that the medieval homilies are mixed prose and verse.

summaries.value: A sequence of twenty-eight Middle English verse homilies edited from the Vernon Manuscript, opening with an extended grain-of-wheat meditation on Christ's Passion and Eucharistic bread and proceeding through feast-day Gospel paraphrase and exposition from the vigil of Saint Andrew to a church dedication. Horstmann's selection preserves six manuscript cross-references but omits one complete repeated Invention of the Cross homily. The Mary Magdalene homily includes six separately headed exempla.

The manuscript sequence has thirty-five feast positions: twenty-nine full homilies and six title-only pointers to homilies already present elsewhere. Horstmann prints twenty-eight of the full homilies in two journal runs and retains the pointers; he omits the repeated full Erat homo homily. The XML represents the resulting twenty-eight-homily edited unit as 5,582 lines in sixty-four verse groups.

This record describes Horstmann's selected scholarly edition and its twenty-eight constituent homilies. The Northern Homily Cycle, Vernon codex, Add MS 22283 parallel, omitted Erat homo homily, and following How a Man Shall Live Perfectly are related but distinct units.

notes: The qualifier makes the edited-selection boundary visible in human-readable browsing. Horstmann's exact title is retained separately.

notes: Proprium Sanctorum is rendered descriptively as Sanctorale; fol. CCXV is Horstmann's Roman-numeral reference to fol. 215.

Horstmann's selection preserves six manuscript cross-references but omits one complete repeated Invention of the Cross homily. The Mary Magdalene homily includes six separately headed exempla.
